findings.description: fetch_events runs a LIMIT-less query and calls .fetchall(), materializing every event in an arbitrary date range into a Python list; render_activity_report then holds that list plus the full concatenated HTML plus the final formatted page at once, so a wide range OOMs the service (fix: paginate or stream the cursor and stream HTML to the output instead of fetchall + one giant string).

The SELECT in fetch_events (lines 35-41) has no LIMIT/pagination, so the result set size is bounded only by how much history the caller selects — the root cause of the unbounded memory and unbounded render loop downstream.

rows += f"..." concatenates onto a growing string inside the per-event loop; CPython's in-place string += optimization is not guaranteed, so this degrades toward O(n^2) reallocation/copy for large reports (fix: append fragments to a list and "".join once, or write directly to a stream).

All queries filter/sort on events.created_at and join on events.user_id (lines 38-39, 67-69, 82, 88); without indexes on those columns the range scans and ORDER BY go full-table, but the schema is out of scope so this is an unverified dependency rather than a defect in this file.

Every public method opens a fresh sqlite3.connect with no pooling/reuse; for SQLite this is cheap and idiomatic so the impact is minor (borderline nit), worth noting only if these methods are called at high frequency per request.
